narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school applications only to face this outcome feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support, with a 44.9% estimated chance of not passing. Picture the model piecing together your story like chapters in a book: your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward potential struggles, suggesting the rigors of advanced legal coursework might have been a tougher climb than for many peers. Right behind that was your 1st decile ranking from Year 1, reinforcing a pattern of early academic hurdles that echoed concerns about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 28 added weight to this narrative, as it positioned you below the thresholds where others typically thrive in bar prep. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.7, factors like your middle family income bracket hinted at fewer resources for the intense tutoring or support that often makes the difference. Your Asian background, while a source of strength for many, aligned in the model's view with heightened competitive pressures that can tip the scales toward failure here. Choosing full-time study, though dedicated, didn't offset these as strongly as hoped. The one bright thread weaving toward success was your gender, which statistically favors passing rates, but it couldn't quite balance the full picture these other elements painted. Together, they told a story of a bright student with real potential, yet facing an uphill battle against these combined headwinds. This isn't a judgment on your worth or drive—it's our way of stewarding spots for those our model sees as safest bets for bar success. If you strengthen those early-grade deciles or LSAT through retakes or targeted prep, you could rewrite this story for next time. You've got this resilience; keep pushing forward.
